summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's official response to the FCC's inquiry about the rejection of Google Voice apps from the App Store. He notes Apple's semantic distinction that the app was 'not approved' rather than 'rejected,' and highlights Apple's stated concern that Google Voice replaces core iPhone functionality like Visual Voicemail and SMS. He points out inconsistencies in Apple's explanation, particularly why third-party Google Voice apps were removed and why other dialer apps remain. Gruber also acknowledges that Apple's claim of acting independently from AT&T contradicts information he previously reported from a source, and he publicly notes that source's unreliability.
key_points: Apple claims it hasn't 'rejected' Google Voice but rather hasn't approved it yet — a semantic distinction Gruber finds notable but insufficient
Apple's stated concern is that Google Voice replaces core iPhone UX for calls, voicemail, and SMS, which Gruber reads as a very Jobs-ian stance about controlling the user experience
The explanation doesn't account for why third-party Google Voice apps were removed or why other dialer/SMS apps remain in the store
Apple explicitly states AT&T played no role in the decision, directly contradicting Gruber's earlier reporting from a source he now flags as unreliable
The App Store review process involves 40+ full-time reviewers handling roughly 8,500 submissions per week, with each app reviewed by at least two people
money_quotes: That's interesting, but it's a bit of semantic hair-splitting.
My reading of this is that Apple's primary problem with Google's Google Voice app is that it redefines the user experience for dialing, voicemail, and SMS. This strikes me as very Jobs-ian.
The only voicemail Apple wants for the iPhone is the built-in Visual Voicemail.

The problem with this explanation is that it doesn't explain (a) why the three other Google Voice-related apps — which were not by Google, and which were already in the App Store — were removed from the App Store; and (b) why other apps which deal with SMS and phone dialing are allowed.
Put another way, Apple does not want to make it easy or seamless for iPhone users to use Google's phone service.
Any future information from this source will be noted accordingly.
key_insight: Apple's explanation for blocking Google Voice reveals a Jobs-ian desire to control the core iPhone experience, but the reasoning has conspicuous holes that suggest competitive motivations against Google.
